title: Multi-Modal Sample Preparation and Imaging Protocol for Nano-to-mesoscopic Mapping of Cellular Inhabitants in Diverse Tissue Compartments, Across Organ Systems

steps: Sample size guidelines The protocol is designed to provide guidelines for preparation of samples that exceed typical sizes used for standard microscopy protocols, expanding the methodologies and associated opportunities for multimodal and correlative imaging to enable nano-to-mesoscopic mapping of cellular inhabitants of diverse tissue compartments, across organ systems and length scales. In previous studies, human femoral neck, and head specimens, obtained from total hip arthroplasty patients, were sectioned into 1 mm thick sections along the plane of interest by the Cleveland Clinic Pathology Department per IRB protocol (#12-335) guidelines and the resulting large scale tissue blocks were imaged, from nm to meso-length scales using multibeam scanning electron microscopy 11,16-20,22,23 . The current study further develops the protocol for multimodal and correlative imaging across length scales using Dunkin Hartley guinea pigs as a model system for age-related tissue degeneration, e.g. associated with osteoarthritis.

Experiment and Specimen preparation (see Figure 1 , first series of blue arrow shapes) Animal experiments were approved by Case Western Reserve University IACUC (Protocol #2012-0100) and were performed in accordance with the ARRIVE guidelines and regulations. In all previously published experiments from our laboratory using the guinea pig model, a bolus of fluorescent tracer was administered via the heart 6-8 . In the current study 7 , a bolus of 70 kDa fluorescent tracer was administered intracardially according to our previously published protocols; two experimental groups either included one of two cytokines, TGF-alpha or TNF-beta, mixed in the bolus of fluorescent tracer. After five minutes' circulation, animals were euthanized and hindlimbs were immediately resected ( Figure 1, Sample Acquisition, Sectioning, and Fixation).

Tissues were immediately either cryopreserved in OCT using liquid nitrogen for Episcopic Cryoimaging ( Figure 1 ) 6-8 or chemically fixed 7 in 2.5% glutaraldehyde and 4% paraformaldehyde in 0.2 M cacodylate buffer for five days. Thereafter, samples were washed with cacodylate buffer daily for another five days. Samples were then dehydrated in a series of ethanols (30%, 50%, 75%, 100% - Figure 1 , Dehydration) for three days each with daily solution changes. After dehydration, samples were embedded in polymethylmethacrylate (PMMA, Figure 1 - Resin Embedding) resin.

Surface Preparation In the present study, samples were micro-CNC milled to obtain a mirror-like finish in areas to be imaged. CNC-milled samples were fixed onto a CNC-lathe and milled with a carbide insert, followed by gentle buffing with an acrylic polish. Then, the samples were gently cleaned with ultra-pure H2O then absolute ethanol with a lint free wipe and were similarly placed under a stream of inert gas. For laboratories that do not have access to micro-CNC milling, machine polishing is an alternative method that we have tested in other studies using a semi-automatic grinder and polisher (Struers LaboPol-5). For machine polishing, specimens are placed, with even pressure, surface-side down on a 500-grit polishing disc at 200 rpm and then cleaned ultrasonically in a beaker of deionized water. This process is repeated with a 1200-grit polishing disc, MD-Dac polishing pad with 3 micron diamond suspension, and then MD-Nap polishing pad with 1 micron diamond suspension. Following the polishing steps, specimens are gently cleaned with absolute ethanol and a lint-free wipe, then placed under a steady stream of inert gas, in this case nitrogen, to ensure they are dry and free of any particulates. After surface preparation, specimens can be directly imaged using confocal imaging and multiphoton imaging modalities (see below). If multimodal imaging is planned, e.g. confocal and electron microscopy imaging, is planned, then it is best to first carry out contrast enhancement, e.g. with chemical etching or iodine staining, prior to confocal imaging and electron microscopy ( Figure 1 ).
Contrast enhancement and viability assay with iodine To enhance contrast and resolve cellular detail using electron microscopy (EM), specimens underwent chemical etching and/or iodine staining.

We used chemical etching to reveal osteocyte networks in calcified tissues, adapting protocols that had been previously implemented for atomic force microscopy of osteocyte lacunocanalicular networks in bone 24 . Specimens were mounted onto a specimen holder using silver paint, carbon, or copper tape, then gently cleaned with absolute ethanol and a lint free wipe. The sample was then placed under a stream of inert gas to ensure it was dry and free for dust. A small volume of 0.02 M hydrochloric acid (HCl) was pipetted onto the specimen surface, then a cotton swab was used to gently expose the sample to the solution for 60 seconds at time, the sample was then flushed with ultra-pure H2O to clear the surface of any debris and residual solution. Following this, a similarly small volume of 2.5% sodium hypochlorite (NaClO) was applied to the specimen in the same manner as the previous step, including flushing with ultra-pure H2O. Specimens were then placed under a steady stream of inert gas to ensure that they were completely dry and free of dust then carbon coated (20nm thickness) in preparation for electron microscopy (EM).

Boyde et al. described the use of iodine staining as an assay for cell viability and we adapted Boyde's protocol for the current study 25,26 . Specimens undergoing iodine staining were cleaned with absolute ethanol and a lint free wipe then placed under a stream of inert gas prior to carbon coating (20 nm thickness). Lugol solution (aqueous potassium iodide and iodine) was then placed on the sample surface for 5 minutes to impart an iodine stain. The excess Lugol solution was then flushing with ultra-pure H2O and the specimen was placed under a steady stream of inert gas.

Multimodal imaging We imaged specimens first using confocal laser scanning microscopy (LSM); for the current study we used a Zeiss LSM 880, an inverted laser scanning confocal microscope equipped with an Argon laser for 488 nm excitation and Helium-Neon (HeNe) laser for 594 excitation, 10Å~ 0.8 Plan-Apochromat dry objective, 20Å~ 0.8 Plan-Apochromat dry objective and 63Å~ 1.4 Plan-Apochromat oil objective to observe tissue autofluorescence and the Texas-red fluorophore.

After completion of confocal imaging, in the current study we then carried out backscattered electron scanning electron microscopy (BSE-SEM) or secondary electron scanning electron microscopy (SEM) using an FEI NanoSEM 450. In previous studies 16-20,22,23 , specimens underwent widefield light microscopy, using a Zeiss Axio Imager Vario, followed by rapid throughput, secondary electron scanning electron microscopy, using a Zeiss multiSEM platform, with up to 91 parallel electron beams.

Results Fixation and Resin Embedding Use of PMMA resin embedding, together with the exclusion of heavy metal (OsO 4 ) staining, enabled the preparation of optically clear specimens conducive to light microscopy. Upon imaging, separation artifacts were observed as empty spaces along tissue compartment boundaries where attached tissues had pulled away from each other, due to shrinkage during chemical fixation 27 . The artifacts were observed at the proximate to the boundary between bone and bone marrow, along bounding fasciae and between muscle and bone; no separation was observed at the osteochondral interface ( Figure 2 ). Surface Preparation While a mirror-like finish is achievable using semi-automatic polishing, some specimens develop micro-cracks on the sample surface. These microcracks often appear at tissue boundaries, causing loss of data, and interfering with both light microscopy and EM imaging quality. Further, using semi- automatic polishing, it is challenging to achieve planarity required for imaging large regions, e.g. for multibeam scanning electron microscopy. CNC-milling, followed by manual buffing, enables both high-precision removal of material as well surface planarity without the development of micro-cracks which interfere with image quality. Chemical Etching Chemical etching is necessary for resolving cellular detail in sample bloscks using SEM. The combined action of dilute sodium hypochlorite and hydrochloric acid enables controlled erosion of the organic and inorganic apatite matrix components, respectively, exposing osteocytes and osteocyte processes embedded in the extracellular matrix (ECM, Figure 3 ). Iodine staining While carbon coating alone provided sufficient contrast to visualize bone and reveal the osteocyte lacuno-canalicular network (LCM) on the sample surface, other tissues remained dark with no discernable features ( Figure 4 ). Lugol solution imparted an iodine stain to musculoskeletal tissues with little to no mineralization, enabling visualization of previously undiscernible musculoskeletal tissues including articular cartilage, calcified cartilage, growth plate cartilage, muscle, periosteum, and bone marrow, in addition to bone. Subcutaneous adipose tissue, arteries and veins were also stained. Osteocytes and the LCM appeared dark against bone with nuclei exhibiting slightly elevated levels of stain in comparison to the cytoplasm. Chondrocytes were stained in a similar manner; however articular cartilage was stained less intensely than bone. Some subcutaneous adipocyte nuclei were observable, appearing brighter than the cytoplasmic rim; no stain was imparted on stored lipids. Marrow cells appeared slightly brighter than the marrow space, bone marrow adipocytes appeared darker than surrounding marrow ( Figure 5 ). Multi-Modal Imaging The initial generation of an overview image of the sample enabled the isolation of regions of interest for further imaging ( Figure 3D ; see Figures in related articles 7,8 ); a low-resolution image is sufficient for this purpose. This also allows for the cross-identification of landmarks for further multi-modal imaging and correlation. The addition of extrinsic fiduciaries to the sample such as a small score to the edge of the specimen aided with navigating large specimens, especially on subsequent imaging modalities. It was important to take note of and record the orientation of the specimen as it is placed onto the microscope stage as well as any changes in rotation in the resultant image. We observed that the resultant laser scanning microscopy and SEM images were mirrored horizontally and rotated 90° clockwise, respectively. Discussion Advances enabling high throughput imaging, such as the multiSEM platform and tiling and stitching software, have made it possible to perform SEM and LSM of specimens up to 10 cm in diameter at nanometer and micron length scales 11,16-20,22,23 . In this present study we expanded upon specimen preparation techniques ordinarily used for TEM and SEM, enabling visualization of tissues from the nano-to-mesoscopic length scales. We adapted chemical etching methods, developed for atomic force microscopy, and previously described iodine staining to facilitate multi-modal and correlative imaging 24-26 . These methods were applied previously to large areas of human tissue (sections of femoral head and neck) and the current protocol highlights their use in tissues of medium-sized vertebrates (guinea pig knee joints including surrounding musculature), demonstrates their feasibility for integration with multi-modal imaging workflows, enabling correlative studies of structure and function spanning multiple length scales. While human tissues present a gold standard for study of human health and mechanisms underpinning disease, intrinsic limitations of human tissue relate to both contexts for ethical implementation and associated lack of experimental controls; while the tissues used in previous studies of the human hip would normally be discarded in the typical course of joint replacement, resection of healthy tissues to serve as a baseline control would be neither ethical nor tenable. As such, animal models offer an alternate pathway for deciphering pathophysiologic mechanisms underpinning disease states and associated loss in tissue/cell structure and function in humans. Pairing animal models with human tissue studies provides a powerful means to understand the human condition in parallel vertebrate animal models, and establishment of methods that apply for both human and animal tissues further amplifies that power. The Dunkin-Hartley guinea pig exhibits age-related spontaneous osteoarthritis which mirrors the condition in humans 28 . Previous studies implementing the Dunkin-Hartley model have demonstrated the functional barrier property of molecular sieving in musculoskeletal tissues and passive transport of large molecules modulated by age and inflammatory state 6-8 . Through cross length scale imaging using multiple imaging modalities – such modalities include episcopic meso to cell-scale imaging of the entire lower limb, high resolution confocal imaging of the same mesoscopic regions in subcellular resolution, and electron microscopy – unprecedented mechanistic relationships were drawn between the cardiovascular and musculoskeletal systems in association with osteoarthritis. In particular, cardiovascular transport from the heart to the musculoskeletal system, could be visualized as a function of molecular size and exposure to immunomodulatory cytokines, using fluorescent probes that cannot be used in humans. Together, these studies provide a new basis of understanding for structure-function relationship between tissues and the cells that inhabit them, across organ systems. Whilst study of single types of molecules, cells, tissues, organs, and organ systems has yielded a plethora of scientific knowledge, new discoveries are predicted to occur at interfaces between organ systems/tissues and through interactions amongst living entities, e.g. cells 11 . Hence, here we expand upon this specimen preparation protocol to enable study of the interplay between and across length scales, organ systems (cardiovascular to musculoskeletal to immune systems), organs, tissues and cells 6-8,23 . The protocol enables unprecedented epidemiological approaches to understand organismal health, be it human or guinea pig, in context of the health of cell populations inhabiting tissues, organs and organ systems. Heavy metal post-fixation staining is typically required to provide sufficient contrast to tissue specimens and to resolve cellular detail for EM 29,30 . The use of heavy metals for contrast enhancement, e.g. typically uranyl acetate, lead citrate and osmium tetroxide, is often a labor-intensive process and requires exposure to extremely hazardous chemicals. Osmium tetroxide imparts an exogenous stain on the entire specimen, reducing optical clarity and hindering light microscopy. Uranyl acetate and lead citrate introduce instability into the specimen, with the risk of precipitation artifact formation over time 31 . The substitution of heavy metal contrast agents for post-hoc chemical etching and iodine staining, in addition to carbon sputtering, provides sufficient contrast and eliminates the use of hazardous chemicals and their associated risks while maintaining sample stability. Chemical fixation of cells and tissues has been shown previously to result in anisotropic shrinkage of cells 27 . In addition, tissue dehydration may result in a 5–70% reduction of tissue volume, resulting in separation artefacts 32 . Dehydration involves organic solvents and is vital to the resin embedding process, as PMMA is not miscible in water. Furthermore, separation artifacts have been observed between bone and bone‐anchored metal implants, suggesting that such artifacts arise at the interface of materials and/or tissues of significantly dissimilar physical properties 33 . This is consistent with our observation of separation artifacts at the tissue boundaries. Separation artifacts caused by dehydration can be reduced by embedding specimens in more water-miscible resins and through the use of cryo-embedding methods such as high-pressure freezing and freeze substitution 34–38. However, it should be noted that while cryo-preservation is a well-established technique for cells and small tissue volumes, cryo-preservation of bulk tissues remains difficult; even with the use of cryo-protective agents, the formation of extracellular ice crystals can disrupt tissue structures 38 . The surface flatness of specimens is vital to EM; defects such as micro-cracks generate edge charging artefacts, causing losses in data, resolution and may result in specimen damage 39 . CNC-milling proved to be the superior method for achieving planarity and a mirror-like finish on the specimen surface. Semi-automated surface preparation proved to be a laborious and time-intensive process. In addition to this, we observed the development of micro-cracks in the sample surface, possibly due to release of residual stresses generated during the resin embedding process. Unlike smaller, conventionally sized, samples, the specimens could not be tilted within the microscopes to level the specimen surface for imaging. While more cautious and slower manual polishing could potentially minimize microcracks, manual surface preparation techniques are not as conducive to achieve the levels of parallelism between the top and bottom surfaces of the sample required for imaging. While the flexibility of this method enables specimens to be imaged using multiple platforms, this same feature makes it not ideal for integrated correlative light and electron microscopy system as the specimen must be removed from the microscope chamber to undergo additional contrast enhancement steps after light microscopy to make it conducive to EM. This protocol enables a streamlined yet flexible approach to specimen preparation of large tissue samples (up to 10cm in diameter). We take a novel approach to materials standard to EM, AFM, and confocal microscopy specimen preparation, visualizing large fields of view without the need for heavy metal staining. The protocol provides a platform for study of structure-function relationships across length scales, from subcellular, cellular, tissue, to organ system. It is ideal for studies involving tissue specimens from human or vertebrate animal models of human health and disease.

Figure 1. Figure 1. Large sample (e.g. medium to large vertebrates) preparation and imaging workflow, from sample acquisition (green) to preparatory steps (blue) for different imaging modalities (orange). Figure 2. Figure 2. Shrinkage artifacts are observable in PMMA embedded specimens observed using confocal microscopy, as denoted by arrow heads (A) near the boundary between bone (gray) and bone marrow (red), (B) along bounding fasciae surrounding a vessel (muscle fibers appearing white) and (C) between the fascia (sheath) around muscle fibers (white) and periosteum sheathing bone (dark gray). (D) Shrinkage artifacts were not observed in the region of the osteochondral interface. Figure 3. Figure 3. Secondary electron SEM of human hip specimens from a previously published study 22,23 comparing (A) etched and unetched areas of tissue. (B) little to no cellular detail is visible in unetched tissues and (C) etching removes matrix components around two osteocytes, which enables imaging of the precise cellular network buried below the previous planar polished surface of the specimen. (D) Overview light microscopy image of entire specimen identifying the region of interest to undergo multibeam scanning electron imaging (here using 61 parallel electron beams), resulting in nano-to-mesoscale images of the hip joint and its cellular inhabitants. (E) Region of interest of the femoral head showing damage to the joint surface, indicated by arrowheads, visualized using the GoogleMaps JavaScript API to create a navigable map. Controlled chemical etching revealed (F) osteocytes under the specimen surface (G), with osteocyte processes extending into the underlying extracellular matrix of the bone. Figure 4. Figure 4. Multi-modal imaging of a region of the femoral diaphysis. (A) Using fluorescence confocal imaging, prior to carbon coating, tissue compartments including muscle, bone and marrow can be readily discerned. (B) Electron microscopy (BSE-SEM) of carbon coated specimens enables visualization of (B) mineralized bone including (C, D) the osteocyte lacunae (indicated by white arrowheads) and canaliculi (dark arrowheads) on the sample surface. All other tissues studied (muscle, marrow) exhibited negligible contrast and tissue boundaries were not discernable. Figure 5. Figure 5. Electron Microscopy (BSE-SEM) image of carbon coated and iodine stained specimens. (A) At the osteochondral interface, osteocytes (indicated by white arrowheads) appear dark against bright bone, calcified cartilage appears slightly lighter than bone. The articular cartilage matrix appears lighter than resident chondrocytes (dark arrowheads). (B) Chondrocytes (white arrowheads) are observable in growth plate cartilage which appears darker than surrounding calcified cartilage and bone. (C) Cross section of a blood vessel within the periosteum reveals red blood cells (indicated by white arrowheads). Howship’s lacunae (craters left behind after osteoclasts resorb bone) are observed in the adjacent bone. (D) Muscle fibers and adipocyte cytoplasmic rims appear similarly bright, and adipocyte nuclei (white arrowheads) are brighter than surrounding cytoplasm (white arrowheads). Also pictured is a musculoskeletal artery showing visual separation of the layers of the vessel wall; red blood cells are observed within the lumen. (E) Similarly, the layers of the vessel wall of a vein can be observed, red blood cells (white arrowheads) appear much brighter than surrounding tissue. (F) The osteocyte lacunocanalicular network (white arrowheads) appears dark against an island of bone (gray) in the marrow space. Marrow cells appear lighter than surrounding marrow tissue; marrow adipocytes appear dark and globular in comparison to other marrow cells (indicated by asterisks).
